MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-30-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Charles Pitt, In Pro Per, (415.368.2354) appears as self-represented plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Renee Erickson Rosenblitt, Deputy City Attorney of the San Francisco City Attorney's Office (Renee.Rosenblit@sfcityatty.org) appears as counsel for moving parties, defendants CCSF, Charles Folger

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides, on record as reflected in the notes of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANTs CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, CHARLES FOLGER's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, Or In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication is granted in part and denied in part.
Summary judgment is granted to sheriff's deputy Folger, because plaintiff Charles Pitts' opposition does not dispute what Folger asserts: that he did not participate in the conduct Pitts complains about. Indeed, the opposition does not mention Folger at all.

Pitts' opposition does aver that other sheriff's deputies (1) "stopped what they were doing pushed plaintiff against wall and started twisting arms and hands when plaintiff moved and yelled in pain then" a deputy "twisted thumb and arm to point of injury" and (2) "Plaintiff was not resisting during time of injury" and deputies "moved plaintiff 30 or more feet before injury." Some courts of appeal say such information cannot be disregarded in ruling on a summary adjudication motion, even when, as here, it is not included in a separate statement filed by an opposing party. (San Diego Watercrafts, Inc. v. Wells Fargo Bank (2002) 102 Cal.App.4th 308; Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 10:95.4-1095.5.)

The opposition creates material disputes of fact (and/or mixed questions of fact and law) as to whether (1) the use of force was reasonable, (2) the detention was lawful and (3) deputies' conduct was extreme or outrageous. Moreover, the City may be vicariously liable for torts of its employees. Thus, the rest of the motion is denied.

As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number.

Written order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR 8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com.

Charles Pitts, Telephone 415-368-2354, appearing on his own behalf in pro per;
Kelly Collins, Esq., Deputy City Attonrey, CCSF, Telepone 415-554-3914, appearing for Defendant CCSF;

RULING - ARGUED: The Court adopts the tentative ruling with modifications as follows:

DEFENDANTS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO and CHARLES FOLGER'S MOTION TO COMPEL ANSWERS TO INTERROGATORIES.

The City and County of San Francisco's motion to compel discovery is GRANTED. On or before February 5, 2019, plaintiff Charles Pitts shall serve verified answers, without objection, to the pending: (1) form interrogatories (Set 1), (2) special interrogatories (Set 1) and (3) requests for production of documents (Set 1). No sanctions are awarded; plaintiff is a low-income pro per litigant. Order is signed.
